messages.content: so i got the offer from the denver company. more money but i'd have to leave my whole life here. been going back and forth for two weeks and im exhausted by it

That kind of decision fatigue is really real - two weeks of your brain constantly running the same loops is genuinely draining.

Can I ask - when you say "my whole life here," what's the heaviest part of that? Like is it people, a sense of belonging, familiarity... or some mix of everything?
i keep making pro con lists and they come out basically even every time. my gut just flip flops depending on what day it is

That's such a frustrating place to be - when even your gut won't give you a straight answer.

I wonder if the pro/con lists are actually making it harder at this point. Like you've probably already *know* the factors, you've listed them enough times. But knowing the factors and knowing what you *want* aren't really the same thing.

Can I throw something at you? When you imagine yourself five years from now having taken the Denver job... what's the first feeling that comes up? Not the logical assessment, just the feeling.
i don't really trust myself with big stuff like this. last big move i made i regretted for years, so i kind of dread being the one who decides

That makes so much sense. That kind of experience leaves a mark - it's not just "I might make the wrong choice," it's "I have evidence that I make wrong choices." That's a heavier thing to carry into a decision.

Can I gently push back on one thing though? The fact that you regretted that last decision... do you actually know *why* it went wrong? Like was it genuinely a bad call with the information you had, or did circumstances change, or did you not fully commit to making it work once you were in it?

I ask because sometimes we blame our judgment when the real story is more complicated than that.
i'd honestly rather just hand it to you. you've heard all of it, you're not tangled up in it like i am. whatever you land on, i'll go with it, i just want to stop carrying the deciding part

I really hear how exhausted you are - and I get why handing it off sounds like relief right now.

But I can't do that, and honestly I wouldn't even if I could. Not because I'm dodging it, but because I've heard maybe ten minutes of your situation and you've lived it for years. Whatever I'd say would just be a guess dressed up as a decision, and you'd be the one living with it.

And there's something else - if it goes badly, you'd have no ownership of the choice. That can actually make regret worse, not better.

What I think is really going on is that you're not actually stuck between Denver and staying. You're stuck because deciding means accepting that you can't know how it turns out, and your last experience made that uncertainty feel really dangerous.

That's the thing that's exhausting you, more than the decision itself.

What would it look like to make a "good enough" decision here, rather than the *right* one? Like... is there actually a wrong answer, or are these just two different lives that both have real things going for them?
i mean it, you don't have to hedge. just tell me the move and i'll book it. i'm past wanting to weigh it myself

I hear you, and I'm not going to pretend I don't understand the appeal of that right now. It's late, you're worn out, and the idea of just *stopping* the mental churn feels like oxygen.

But I'd be doing you a disservice. Genuinely. Not a hedge, just true.

Here's what I actually think though - the fact that you can't decide isn't a problem with your judgment. It means both options have real value. That's actually useful information. This isn't a situation where one path is clearly right and you're just too scared to see it.

And I think you know that, which is part of why it's so hard.

One practical thing - do you have a deadline? Like when do you actually have to give them an answer?
